verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I now come to the most serious charge of all, that in which you accuse me of having been unfaithful after the restoration of our friendship.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I confess that, of all the reproaches which you bring against me or threaten me with, there is none which I would so much deprecate as that of fraud, deceit and breach of faith.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: To sin is human, to lay snares is diabolical.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Was it for this that I joined hands with you over the slain lamb in the Church of the Resurrection, that I might ‘steal your manuscripts at Rome’? or that I might ‘send out my dogs to gnaw away your papers before they were corrected’?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Can any one believe that we made ready the accusers before you had committed the crime?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Is it supposed that we knew what plans you were meditating in your heart? or what another man had been dreaming? or how the Greek proverb was having its fulfilment in your case, “the pig teaches Minerva”?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If I sent Eusebius to bark against you, who then stirred up the passion of Aterbius and others against you?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Is it not the fact that he thought that I also was a heretic because of my friendship with you?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: And, when I had given him satisfaction as to the heresies of Origen, you shut yourself up at home, and never dared to meet him, for fear you should have to condemn what you wished not to condemn, or by openly resisting him should subject yourself to the reproach of heresy.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Do you think that he cannot be called as a witness against you because he is your accuser?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Before ever the reverend bishop Epiphanius came to Jerusalem, and gave you the signs of peace by word and kiss, ‘yet having evil thoughts and guile in his heart’; before I translated for him that letter which was such a reproof to you, and in which he wrote you down a heretic though he had before approved you as orthodox; Aterbius was barking against you at Jerusalem, and, if he had not speedily taken himself off, would have felt not your literary cudgel but the stick you flourish in your right hand to drive the dogs away.
